name: Auschwitz-Birkenau
note: Largest Nazi concentration and extermination camp in occupied Poland.

name: Treblinka
note: Extermination camp in occupied Poland where ~870,000 Jews were murdered.

name: Sobibor
note: Extermination camp in occupied Poland, site of prisoner uprising in 1943.

name: Belzec
note: First Operation Reinhard extermination camp; ~500,000 Jews killed.

name: Chelmno (Kulmhof)
note: First Nazi extermination camp, where victims were murdered in gas vans.

name: Majdanek (Lublin)
note: Concentration and extermination camp near Lublin, Poland.

name: Bergen-Belsen
note: Concentration camp in Germany, site of mass deaths from starvation and disease.

name: Dachau
note: First Nazi concentration camp, near Munich, Germany.

name: Sachsenhausen
note: Concentration camp north of Berlin, used as a model for other camps.

name: Ravensbrück
note: Camp primarily for women, located in northern Germany.

name: Mauthausen
note: Austrian camp notorious for forced labor in quarries.

name: Theresienstadt (Terezín)
note: Czech ghetto-camp used for propaganda and as a transit camp.

name: Assyrian deportations from the Kingdom of Israel
note: Assyrian campaigns deported populations from the northern Kingdom of Israel, including Galilee, Transjordan, and Samaria.

name: Babylonian exile from Jerusalem
note: Jewish elites and many inhabitants of Judah were deported to Babylon following the Babylonian conquest and destruction of Jerusalem.

name: Expulsion of Jews from Rome
note: Jews were expelled from Rome under accusations of proselytising among Romans.

name: Expulsion of Jews from Rome under Tiberius
note: Emperor Tiberius expelled Jews from Rome, together with practitioners of Egyptian religion.

name: Expulsion from Jewish quarter in Alexandria
note: Jews were expelled from one of their quarters in Alexandria under Aulus Avilius Flaccus.

name: Claudius' expulsion of Jews from Rome
note: Jews were expelled from Rome during the reign of Claudius.

name: Displacement after the First Jewish-Roman War
note: The Roman suppression of the Jewish revolt led to destruction, enslavement, and widespread displacement from Judaea.

name: Expulsion after the Diaspora Revolt
note: Suppression of the Diaspora Revolt led to destruction and expulsion of Jewish communities in Cyrenaica, Cyprus, and Egypt.

name: Expulsions after the Bar Kokhba revolt
note: After the Bar Kokhba revolt, Jews were expelled from the vicinity of Jerusalem and other districts of Judaea.

name: Expulsion of Jews from Alexandria
note: Jews were expelled from Alexandria under Cyril of Alexandria, following conflict between Jews and Christians.

name: Expulsion or forced conversion of Jews in Minorca
note: Jews in Minorca were expelled or pressured to convert.

name: Visigothic persecution and expulsions in Iberia
note: Visigothic rulers in Iberia imposed repeated anti-Jewish laws, forced conversions, and expulsions.

name: Expulsion from Mainz
note: Jews were expelled from Mainz during the early medieval period.

name: Expulsion from France under Philip Augustus
note: Philip II Augustus ordered the expulsion of Jews from the royal domain of France.

name: Expulsion from Brittany
note: Jews were expelled from Brittany.

name: Expulsion from Gascony and Anjou
note: Jews were expelled from Gascony and Anjou under English rule.

name: Expulsion from England
note: Edward I issued the Edict of Expulsion, expelling Jews from England.

name: Expulsion from France under Philip IV
note: Philip IV expelled Jews from France and confiscated Jewish property.

name: Expulsion from France under Charles VI
note: Charles VI ordered another expulsion of Jews from France.

name: Expulsion from Vienna and Austria
note: The Vienna Gesera led to persecution, execution, forced conversion, and expulsion of Jews from Austria.

name: Expulsion from Cologne
note: Jews were expelled from Cologne.

name: Expulsion from Augsburg
note: Jews were expelled from Augsburg in the fifteenth century.

name: Expulsion from Bavaria
note: Jews were expelled from parts of Bavaria.

name: Expulsion from Moravia
note: Jews were expelled from Moravia.

name: Expulsion from Perugia
note: Jews were expelled from Perugia.

name: Expulsion from Spain
note: The Alhambra Decree ordered the expulsion of practising Jews from Spain unless they converted.

name: Expulsion from Sicily
note: Jews were expelled from Sicily following the Spanish expulsion decrees.

name: Expulsion from Portugal
note: Portugal ordered the expulsion or forced conversion of Jews.

name: Expulsion from Navarre
note: Jews were expelled from Navarre.

name: Expulsion from Naples
note: Jews were expelled from the Kingdom of Naples.

name: Expulsion from Provence
note: Jews were expelled from Provence after its annexation to France.

name: Expulsion from Genoa
note: Jews were expelled from Genoa.

name: Expulsion from Papal States except Rome and Ancona
note: Pope Pius V ordered Jews expelled from most of the Papal States, except Rome and Ancona.

name: Expulsion from Brandenburg
note: Jews were expelled from Brandenburg.

name: Expulsion from Hamburg
note: Jews were expelled from Hamburg.

name: Expulsion from Vienna
note: Leopold I expelled Jews from Vienna and Lower Austria.

name: Expulsion from Prague
note: Maria Theresa ordered Jews expelled from Prague and Bohemia; many later returned.

name: Expulsion from Jaffa and Tel Aviv
note: Ottoman authorities expelled residents, including Jews, from Jaffa and Tel Aviv during the First World War.

name: Nazi-era expulsions and forced flight of Jews from Germany
note: Nazi persecution drove large-scale Jewish emigration, expulsion, and forced flight from Germany before and during the Holocaust.

name: Expulsion and flight of Jews from Arab and Muslim countries
note: Hundreds of thousands of Jews left, fled, or were expelled from Arab and Muslim-majority countries in the twentieth century.

name: Expulsion of Jews from Egypt
note: Following the Suez Crisis, many Jews were expelled from Egypt or forced to leave.

name: Flight and expulsion of Jews from Iraq
note: Most Iraqi Jews left during Operation Ezra and Nehemiah after escalating persecution and denaturalisation.

name: Expulsion of Jews from Libya
note: Following anti-Jewish violence during the Six-Day War period, most remaining Jews left Libya.

name: Expulsion from Naples
note: Jews were expelled from Naples.

name: Expulsion from Dubrovnik
note: Jews were expelled from Dubrovnik, with exceptions for physicians and short stays by merchants.

name: Expulsion from Regensburg
note: Jews were expelled from Regensburg.

name: Expulsion from Pressburg
note: Jews were expelled from Pressburg, now Bratislava, after the defeat of the Kingdom of Hungary by the Ottoman Empire.

name: Expulsion from Bavaria
note: All remaining Jews were expelled from the Duchy of Bavaria.

name: Expulsion from the Papal States
note: Pope Pius V expelled Jews from the Papal States, except for Ancona and Rome.

name: Expulsion from the Papal States under Clement VIII
note: Pope Clement VIII expelled Jews from the Papal States, except Rome, Avignon, and Ancona.

name: Expulsion from Milan
note: Nine hundred Jews were expelled from Milan.

name: Fettmilch Uprising and expulsion from Frankfurt
note: Jews were expelled from Frankfurt following the Fettmilch Uprising and the plundering of the Judengasse.

name: Jewish flight from Recife
note: The fall of Dutch Recife to the Portuguese prompted Jewish flight, including the group that later arrived in New Amsterdam.

name: Expulsion from Vienna and Austrian hereditary lands
note: Jews were expelled from Vienna by Leopold I and forbidden to settle in the Austrian hereditary lands.

name: Mawza Exile in Yemen
note: Jews throughout Yemen were expelled from their towns and villages and sent to a desert region in the Mawza Exile.

name: Expulsion from French Caribbean territories
note: Jews were expelled from French colonial Caribbean territories by Louis XIV's Code Noir.

name: Expulsion of Austrian-origin Jews from Bavaria
note: After the War of the Spanish Succession, Jews of Austrian origin were expelled from Bavaria, though some later acquired residence rights in Munich.

name: Migration eastward of impoverished German and Austrian Jews
note: Reforms under Frederick II, Joseph II, and Maria Theresa sent many impoverished German and Austrian Jews eastward.

name: Creation of the Pale of Settlement
note: Catherine the Great instituted the Pale of Settlement, restricting Jews to the western parts of the Russian Empire by means of deportation and residence limits.

name: General Order No. 11
note: Jews were expelled from parts of Tennessee, Mississippi, and Kentucky under Ulysses S. Grant's General Order No. 11.

name: Russian Empire pogroms and mass emigration
note: Pogroms in the Russian Empire prompted mass Jewish emigration from eastern Europe, especially to the United States.

name: Expulsion from Jaffa
note: Ottoman authorities expelled Jews from the Jaffa area during World War I.

name: Nazi persecution and Jewish refugee crisis
note: Nazi persecution, beginning in 1933 and culminating in the Holocaust, created a major Jewish refugee and displaced persons crisis in Europe.

name: Soviet June Deportation of Polish Jews
note: The Soviet June Deportation from occupied eastern Poland also targeted Polish Jews who had fled from German-occupied Poland.

name: Expulsions and citizenship stripping in Italian cities
note: Jews were expelled, stripped of citizenship, and subjected to pogroms in several Italian cities, including Rome, Verona, Florence, Pisa, and Alessandria.

name: Jewish exodus from Arab and Muslim countries
note: Jewish communities across the Middle East and North Africa declined sharply through flight, expulsion, persecution, and emigration.

name: Egyptian Companies' Law affecting Jews
note: Egypt passed a Companies' Law requiring most employees to be Egyptian citizens, strongly affecting Jews because many lacked citizenship.

name: Anti-Jewish persecution and emigration from Egypt
note: After the establishment of Israel, antisemitism in Egypt intensified; arrests, confiscations, bombings, and looting contributed to Jewish emigration.

name: Jordanian rule in the West Bank
note: Jordan occupied and annexed the West Bank, imposing discrimination and persecution on non-Muslim residents, including Jews.

name: Operation Ezra and Nehemiah
note: Around 120,000 Iraqi Jews were displaced or expelled under the De-Naturalization Act during Operation Ezra and Nehemiah.

name: Nasser-era persecution of Jews in Egypt
note: Under Gamal Abdel Nasser, many Jews were arrested, Jewish enterprises were supervised, confiscated, or forcibly sold, and communal life deteriorated.

name: Suez Crisis expulsions from Egypt
note: During and after the Suez Crisis, thousands of Egyptian Jews were interned or ordered to leave, often without property or capital.

name: Jewish flight from Algeria
note: Jews fled Algeria amid FLN violence and fears surrounding independence; most left for France or Israel.

name: Deterioration of Jewish life in Algeria
note: The situation of Jews in Algeria rapidly deteriorated, and by 1969 fewer than 1,000 Jews remained.

name: Six-Day War arrests and expulsions from Egypt
note: After the Six-Day War, hundreds of Egyptian Jews were arrested and many were later permitted or forced to leave.

name: Mass exodus of Libyan Jews
note: Anti-Jewish violence in Libya during the Six-Day War period prompted a mass exodus, leaving fewer than 100 Jews in the country.

name: Polish anti-Zionist campaign
note: Thousands of Jews were forced to leave communist Poland during the anti-Zionist campaign of the 1968 Polish political crisis.

name: Final decline of Jewish population in Egypt
note: By 1970 fewer than 1,000 Jews remained in Egypt; Jews were allowed to leave but not take their possessions.

name: Soviet Jewish emigration and Refuseniks
note: State-sponsored persecution in the Soviet Union prompted hundreds of thousands of Soviet Jews, including Refuseniks, to seek emigration.

name: Idi Amin expels Israelis from Uganda
note: Idi Amin expelled Israelis from Uganda.

name: Operation Moses and Operation Joshua
note: Around 10,000 Ethiopian Jews fled Ethiopia as part of Operation Moses and Operation Joshua.

name: Operation Solomon
note: Around 14,000 Ethiopian Jews fled Ethiopia as part of Operation Solomon.

name: Last Jew leaves Libya
note: The last Jew left Libya.

name: Israeli disengagement from Gaza
note: The Israeli disengagement from the Gaza Strip forced the relocation of around 8,000 people.

name: Loss of contact with last Jews in Somalia
note: Contact with the last two Jews in Somalia was lost.

name: Last Jews leave Afghanistan
note: The last Jews left Afghanistan.

name: Near-disappearance of the Jewish community in Yemen
note: The Jewish population of Yemen decreased from 18 to six in 2021 and later to about five by 2024.

name: Warsaw Ghetto Deportations
note: Mass deportations from Warsaw Ghetto to Treblinka during Grossaktion Warsaw (approx. 265,000 Jews).

name: Łódź Ghetto Deportations
note: Deportations from Łódź (Litzmannstadt) Ghetto primarily to Chełmno extermination camp and later to Auschwitz.

name: Vilna Ghetto Deportations
note: Deportations from Vilna to Ponary forest, site of mass shootings where ~70,000 Jews were murdered.

name: Theresienstadt Deportations
note: Deportations from Theresienstadt to Auschwitz; many elderly Jews sent to Theresienstadt as 'model ghetto' before extermination.

name: Kovno Ghetto Deportations
note: Deportations from Kovno (Kaunas) Ghetto to Ninth Fort and later Auschwitz. Thousands murdered at Ninth Fort shootings.

name: Salonika Deportations
note: Jews of Thessaloniki (Salonika, Greece) deported to Auschwitz. Around 45,000–50,000 murdered upon arrival.

name: Amsterdam Deportations
note: Deportations from Amsterdam via Westerbork transit camp to Auschwitz and Sobibor. Anne Frank among those deported.

name: Paris Drancy Deportations
note: Deportations from Drancy transit camp near Paris to Auschwitz. Over 67,000 French Jews deported.

name: Budapest Deportations
note: Deportations of Hungarian Jews in 1944, primarily to Auschwitz; over 400,000 Jews deported in just two months.

name: Prague Deportations
note: Deportations of Prague Jews via Theresienstadt, then onward to Auschwitz and other extermination camps.

name: Boycott of all Jewish Shops
note: Boycott of all Jewish shops in Berlin. In October, all hospitals declared 'free' of Jewish doctors.

name: Warsaw Ghetto
note: The Warsaw Ghetto was established and liquidated during WWII.

name: Prague
note: 18,000 (8% of the population

name: Odesa Pogrom
note: One of the earliest pogroms; Jews accused of involvement in the death of the Greek Orthodox patriarch.

name: Odesa Pogroms
note: A series of violent attacks on Jews in Odesa, including the major 1871 pogrom.

name: Kiev Pogrom
note: Massive pogrom following assassination of Tsar Alexander II; hundreds of Jewish homes and shops destroyed.

name: Warsaw Pogrom
note: Anti-Jewish riot in Warsaw, then part of the Russian Empire.

name: Balta Pogrom
note: Pogrom in Podolia Governorate, causing mass destruction of Jewish property.

name: Chişinău Pogrom
note: Also known as the Kishinev Pogrom; 49 Jews killed, hundreds wounded, property destroyed.

name: Homel Pogrom
note: Anti-Jewish violence in Homel (now Belarus) after disputes with Russian nationalists.

name: Białystok Pogrom
note: More than 80 Jews murdered; instigated by local Russian authorities.

name: Kiev Pogroms
note: During Russian Civil War, thousands of Jews murdered in Ukraine in a wave of pogroms by various armies.

name: Lwów Pogrom
note: Following Polish takeover of Lwów, hundreds of Jews killed, synagogues and homes burned.

name: Hebron Massacre
note: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1929_Hebron_massacre

name: Warsaw Ghetto
note: The Warsaw Ghetto was established and liquidated during WWII.

name: כסף נבחר R' Yoshiyahu Pinto
note: A collection of d'rashot on Bereishit and Shemot.

name: כסף מזוקק R' Yoshiyahu Pinto
note: A homiletic commentary on the Torah, followed by a pamphlet entitled Kesef To'afot, glosses on the Torah

name: מאור עיניים R' Yoshiyahu Pinto
note: A commentary on R'Yakov ibn Habib's Ein Yaakov on the Agaddah sections of the Gemara

name: R' Yoshiyahu Pinto postumous sefarim and works
note:
• כסף צורף – commentary on Mishle
  • נבחר מכסף – Responsa
  • כסף נמס– commentary on Eichah
  • קבוצת כסף - works on halacha

name: 12000 Jewish German soldiers die
note: Of 100,000 Jews serving in the German army, 12000 die in the first world war.

name: First Aliyah
note: Wave of Jewish immigration to Ottoman Palestine, mostly from Eastern Europe and Yemen; established agricultural settlements like Rishon LeZion, Petah Tikva, and Zikhron Yaakov.

name: Publication of Der Judenstaat
note: Theodor Herzl published 'Der Judenstaat' in Vienna, laying the ideological foundation for political Zionism.

name: First Zionist Congress
note: Held in Basel. Theodor Herzl convened the first congress, established the World Zionist Organization, adopted the Basel Program.

name: Second Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Established the Jewish Colonial Trust, the financial arm of the Zionist movement.

name: Third Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Focused on organizational matters and support for settlement activity in Palestine.

name: Fourth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Known as the 'Culture Congress.' Debated the role of Hebrew culture and education in Zionism.

name: Fifth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Created the Jewish National Fund (Keren Kayemet LeYisrael) to purchase land in Palestine.

name: Sixth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. The 'Uganda Proposal' for temporary Jewish settlement in East Africa was presented, causing heated controversy.

name: Second Aliyah
note: Immigration wave bringing socialist pioneers from Eastern Europe; foundation of the kibbutz movement and Hashomer defense organization.

name: Seventh Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Uganda Proposal officially rejected; movement reaffirmed Palestine as the goal.

name: Eighth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Marked shift to practical Zionism, focusing on settlement and Hebrew education.

name: Ninth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Chaim Weizmann began to rise as a leading Zionist figure.

name: Tel Aviv Founded
note: Jewish neighborhood Ahuzat Bayit established outside Jaffa, later becoming Tel Aviv.

name: Tenth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Continued debates between political and practical Zionists.

name: Eleventh Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Last congress before World War I; debates on education and Hebrew culture.

name: Balfour Declaration
note: British Foreign Secretary Arthur Balfour declared support for a 'national home for the Jewish people' in Palestine.

name: San Remo Conference
note: Allied powers assigned the Mandate for Palestine to Britain, incorporating the Balfour Declaration.

name: League of Nations Mandate for Palestine
note: League of Nations formally approved the British Mandate for Palestine, recognizing Jewish national aspirations.

name: Twelfth Zionist Congress
note: Carlsbad. First post–WWI congress; endorsed Balfour Declaration and British Mandate.

name: Thirteenth Zionist Congress
note: Carlsbad. Chaim Weizmann emphasized practical work in Palestine; tensions grew with Revisionist Zionists.

name: Fourteenth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Marked the inauguration of Hebrew University in Jerusalem. Weizmann re-elected President.

name: Hebrew University Opened
note: Inauguration of Hebrew University on Mount Scopus, Jerusalem, attended by Chaim Weizmann and Lord Balfour.

name: Fifteenth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Discussed economic challenges facing the Yishuv and immigration.

name: Sixteenth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Debates following Arab riots in Palestine (Hebron massacre).

name: Seventeenth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Zionist Revisionists under Jabotinsky walked out; Weizmann resigned as President.

name: Eighteenth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. First after Nazi rise to power in Germany. Focused on rescue and immigration.

name: Nineteenth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Chaim Weizmann returned as leader. Emphasis on Jewish refugee crisis in Europe.

name: Twentieth Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Debated the British Peel Commission proposal for partition of Palestine. Movement split on acceptance.

name: Twenty-First Zionist Congress
note: Basel. Opposed British 1939 White Paper restricting Jewish immigration, especially as Holocaust began.

name: White Paper of 1939
note: British policy severely limiting Jewish immigration to Palestine on the eve of the Holocaust.

name: Twenty-Second Zionist Congress
note: Basel. First after the Holocaust. Declared demand for a Jewish state in Palestine.
